explanation.text: After more than a year of confusing symbols, Grotjan suddenly notices that some appear at a regular interval and shows the pattern to her team. Their immediate celebration introduces the breakthrough by explaining the interesting relationship she detects.

The paragraphs place Purple within World War II, Japan's alliance with Germany and Italy, and worsening U.S.–Japan relations. This dangerous international context explains why reading secret Japanese communications was important.

Paragraphs 8 through 10 detail a complex chain: intercepting radio messages, sending them to Washington, solving changing symbols through guesses, constructing a matching machine, and translating the result. This extensive evidence best supports the claim that the United States used a complex system to intercept and break codes.

Each symbol's meaning changes as the machine advances, causing one symbol to represent different letters, so analysts must repeatedly guess and test likely phrases. These shifting rules and repeated checks illustrate why decoding the messages was difficult.

Paragraph 11 explains that the reconstructed machine lets officials read Japan's secret diplomatic messages and learn its wartime actions and plans. Sending that information to leaders such as President Roosevelt demonstrates the decoded messages' value to the government.
The illustration depicts a cipher machine similar to the one the article describes and labels it as such. It therefore helps readers visualize the device used to read coded messages.

A government committee credits the decoded "Magic" messages with helping defeat the enemy, shortening the war, and saving thousands of lives. Those enormous outcomes most clearly demonstrate the importance of Grotjan's discovery.

The authors say a one-room schoolhouse classroom probably resembled a modern classroom, even though it used a raised desk, wood stove, and outdoor bathroom. This comparison shows that present-day schools retain some connection to those of the past.

Modern children at Honeoye Falls wear nineteenth-century clothing and learn for a week as students did more than a century earlier. This direct reenactment connects today's students with the experience of children from another time.

Rochester has held a Lilac Festival every May since 1898, continuing a celebration rooted in Highland Park's historic lilac collection. The recurring modern event shows how a tradition can honor the past while celebrating in the present.

The Montauk Point Lighthouse has guided many kinds of ships for more than two hundred years and continues performing that service today. This ongoing help to ships most clearly indicates its lasting importance.

Each section explains a historical place or development and then shows how people preserve, reenact, celebrate, or learn from it today. Connecting the original history with its continuing tradition develops the article's idea of a legacy.

In a lichen, the algae or bacteria makes food for itself and its fungal partner, while the fungus supplies minerals and shelter. This mutual exchange best supports the claim that a lichen functions as a team.

Potter paints fungi in their exact natural settings, labels features that distinguish species, and sketches sprouting spores every six hours under magnification. This careful attention to small changes and identifying details supports the claim that she possessed exceptional observation.

McIntosh sends Potter unusual fungal specimens to paint, giving her direct access to varied study material that advances her art and investigation. His supply of specimens therefore has the most important practical influence on her work.

Potter reports seeing fungi apparently by the thousands and finding more than twenty kinds within minutes. This abundance explains that the fungus "starred the ground" by appearing plentifully across it.

Potter records natural settings, distinguishes species, experiments with growing fungi, magnifies spores, and sketches their changes at regular intervals. These persistent investigations reveal her curiosity about how the natural world works.

Potter's lichen paper and meetings receive little scientific attention, while her children's stories are becoming popular. She responds by focusing on writing and illustrating books, which leads to her fame as a children's author.
To group lichens with fungi means scientists placed them in the same scientific category. This classification indicates that scientists believed lichens and fungi were related.
